summary: Big day for the Southeast BeltLine with new developments breaking ground in Reynoldstown and West End finally taking shape. Meanwhile, Atlanta's dealing with airport chaos as TSA agents work without pay and ICE helps out with security.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown BeltLine Townhomes Finally Break Ground After 5-Year Wait
topStory.summary: Construction is officially underway on that corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace that's been sitting empty since 2021. Embry Development is taking over from Toll Brothers to build 85 residential units just a block from the Eastside Trail. This has been a long time coming for neighbors who've been watching that cleared lot wondering what would happen next.
topStory.body:
Remember that corner in Reynoldstown where Chester Avenue meets Fulton Terrace? The one that's been an empty lot for nearly five years after the old buildings got torn down? Well, finally some good news. Construction equipment rolled in this month as Embry Development officially started work on what they're calling Camber Crossing.

The Atlanta-based company bought the 2-acre site from national developer Toll Brothers two years ago and is moving forward with the permitted plan for 85 residential units. We're talking townhomes and condos about a block west of the BeltLine's Eastside Trail, which as you know is basically real estate gold these days. The original plans got approved back when this was still a Toll Brothers project, but Embry's team says they're keeping the same vision.

This is exactly the kind of missing middle housing the neighborhood needs. Close enough to the BeltLine that you can walk to Ponce City Market or grab dinner in Inman Park, but still maintaining that residential feel that makes Reynoldstown special. The timing couldn't be better either, with the BeltLine continuing to drive development all along the corridor.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Camber Crossing development site in Reynoldstown

The Atlanta-based company bought the 2-acre site from national developer Toll Brothers two years ago and is moving forward with the permitted plan for 85 residential units. stories.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Camber Crossing development site in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Finally Taking Shape
stories.summary: Those townhomes at 600 Hopkins Street are actually starting to look like something. Ackerman & Co. started demo a year ago and now the first seven units of what they're calling GW Parkside are almost ready for market. These are some of the rare for-sale options along the Southwest BeltLine stretch.
stories.body:
You know that spot at 600 Hopkins Street where Ackerman & Co. has been working on townhomes? Well, they're finally starting to rise up and look like actual houses. The developer (same folks behind Lee + White and all those cool office and food spots) renamed the project GW Parkside and the first phase is coming along nicely.

We're talking 23 new units total, with the first seven hitting the market this spring. This is pretty significant for the West End because there just aren't that many for-sale options along this stretch of the BeltLine. Most of the development has been rental apartments, so having townhomes you can actually buy is a big deal for folks who want to put down roots in the area.

The location couldn't be better either. You're right on the Southwest BeltLine Corridor, walking distance to Lee + White for coffee or lunch, and still close enough to downtown. Plus the West End has been seeing a lot of investment lately, so getting in now while you still can makes sense.
stories.imageAlt: GW Parkside townhomes under construction in West End

stories.headline: Decatur Gets New Missing Middle Housing as Prices Soar
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is launching next week with 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards. The goal is more accessible single-family housing near downtown Decatur, where median home values have hit $700,000. This village-style development is replacing underused lots on Midway Road.
stories.body:
With median home values in Decatur pushing past $700,000, finding anything remotely affordable ITP has become pretty much impossible. That's why Cottages at Midway caught my attention. This mixed-income community at 2883 and 2893 Midway Road is trying to crack the code on more accessible housing.

The developers are going for a village feel with 18 standalone houses arranged around shared courtyards and green space. It's that missing middle housing everyone talks about but rarely builds. Instead of massive apartment complexes or million-dollar McMansions, you get actual houses that regular people might actually afford. They're doing a soft launch next week to start drumming up interest.

This is exactly what we need more of ITP. The location works too, you're close to that major commercial corridor and downtown Decatur but still on a quiet residential street. If they can pull this off and keep the prices reasonable, it could be a model for other neighborhoods dealing with the same affordability crunch.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan for Cottages at Midway development in Decatur

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. Think soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis. After years of pop-ups, they've finally got a permanent home in the neighborhood.
stories.body:
After watching Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez do pop-ups around town for the past couple years, it's exciting to see them finally land a permanent spot. Mushi Ni opened yesterday on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park, literally steps from the Freedom Park Trail, which is perfect timing with spring weather bringing folks back outside.

The menu is exactly what you'd hope for, Asian-American comfort food that's both familiar and surprising. They've got soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, crab Rangoon puffs, and those Peking chicken platters that feed two people for $25. The cocktail program looks solid too, with drinks like tamarind whiskey sour and Thai tea martinis that actually sound interesting rather than gimmicky.

Both chefs have serious credentials, Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, and Le has been building a following here for years. The location is smart too, you've got all the Inman Park foot traffic plus people using the trail. It's exactly the kind of independent, chef-driven spot the neighborhood needs more of.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style BBQ to Ansley Mall
stories.summary: The smokehouse at Lewis Barbecue behind Cook's Warehouse is unlike anything you've seen. Six 22-foot smokers in a glass-walled loft overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. Pitmaster John Lewis is turning out some of the best brisket in Atlanta, running those smokers 24/7.
stories.body:
I've been to a lot of barbecue joints, but I've never seen anything quite like the setup at Lewis Barbecue. They put their smokehouse upstairs in a spacious loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. Six massive 22-foot smokers that John Lewis helped weld himself out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, running 24 hours a day in eight-hour shifts.

It's like the fanciest engine room in Georgia, but what really matters is the food. Lewis helped define Austin barbecue before moving to Charleston, and now he's bringing that Texas-by-way-of-Charleston style to Atlanta. The brisket is legitimately some of the best you'll find here, with that perfect smoke ring and bark that only comes from obsessive technique.

The location at Ansley Mall works perfectly too. You're right there behind The Cook's Warehouse with easy access to the BeltLine, which means you can grab some world-class barbecue and take it for a walk. Between this and Ponce City Market, that stretch of the BeltLine is becoming a serious food destination.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's New Food Hall Opens in April Near East Lake
stories.summary: The guy behind New York's Gansevoort Market is opening his third Atlanta food hall at Vela Park near East Lake Golf Club. Vela Park Food Works will have six chef-driven concepts plus craft cocktails, with a mid-April debut planned. This is becoming quite the food destination on the eastside.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid knows food halls. The founder of New York City's legendary Gansevoort Market has already given us two solid spots in Atlanta, and now he's going for a third. Vela Park Food Works is finishing construction at the new Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club, and they're planning a mid-April opening.

This isn't just another generic food court. Montwaid is bringing six different chef-driven concepts together with craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor dining space. The guy has a track record of finding real talent and giving them a platform, so this could be something special. The location makes sense too, that whole area around East Lake has been quietly developing into a food scene.

What I like about Montwaid's approach is that he gives chefs room to do their thing while handling all the infrastructure and marketing. It's a model that's worked well at his other Atlanta locations, and the eastside has been waiting for something like this. Between this and all the other development happening around there, East Lake is definitely having a moment.
stories.imageAlt: Interior construction view of Vela Park Food Works

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Makeover Nearly Done on West Howard
stories.summary: That decade-long project to transform West Howard Avenue from a drag strip into an actual Complete Street is almost finished. New sidewalks, a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, and traffic calming from Commerce Drive to the Atlanta city limits. It's a huge improvement for getting around without a car.
stories.body:
If you've driven down West Howard Avenue lately, you've probably noticed all the construction. Well, it's almost over, and the results look pretty impressive. Decatur has been working on this Complete Streets project for nearly a decade, and they're finally wrapping up the roughly one-mile stretch between Downtown Decatur and Atlanta's city limits.

The big addition is a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers running from Commerce Drive near Decatur High School all the way to Paden Circle by East Lake MARTA station. They've also upgraded sidewalks, added better lighting, and put in traffic calming measures to slow down cars. It's exactly the kind of infrastructure we need more of if we're serious about getting people out of cars.

This connects to so much other bike infrastructure too. You can ride from downtown Decatur all the way to the BeltLine now, or hop on MARTA at East Lake. It's the kind of project that takes forever to get done but makes a huge difference once it's finished. Decatur has been ahead of the curve on this stuff for years.
stories.imageAlt: New cycle track with concrete barriers on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls While Fire Union Contract Stalls
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward two resolutions to limit ICE operations in Atlanta, while dozens of residents criticized Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighter union contract that council already approved. Both ICE measures head to full council vote April 20th.
stories.body:
City Council's Public Safety Committee had their hands full Monday night, dealing with both immigration enforcement and labor issues. They unanimously advanced two resolutions aimed at controlling ICE operations in the city. One would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits, the other requires Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations.

But the bigger drama was over the firefighter contract situation. Dozens of Atlantans showed up to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever contract with the firefighter union, even though council ratified it back in April 2025. The crowd was pretty fired up about it, with many questioning why the mayor won't support the agreement council already approved.

Both ICE resolutions will go to the full council for a vote on April 20th, and given the unanimous committee support, they'll probably pass. The firefighter contract issue is messier and doesn't have an obvious resolution path. It's rare to see this kind of direct conflict between the mayor and council, especially on something council has already voted on.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting

stories.headline: ICE Agents Deployed to Atlanta Airport as Shutdown Creates Chaos
stories.summary: Armed federal agents in tactical gear are patrolling Hartsfield-Jackson as the government shutdown forces TSA agents to work without pay. Wait times hit four hours Monday, with nearly 3,500 TSA workers calling out sick nationwide. The agents aren't doing immigration enforcement, just providing security support.
stories.body:
The scene at Hartsfield-Jackson yesterday was pretty surreal. Armed ICE agents in tactical gear walking around the terminals while thousands of travelers waited in lines stretching for hours. It's all because of the partial government shutdown that's left TSA agents working without pay since mid-February, and a lot of them are just not showing up.

The airport was telling people to arrive four hours early, which is absolutely insane for domestic travel. Nearly 3,500 TSA workers called out nationwide on Monday alone, the highest rate since the shutdown started. The ICE agents Mayor Dickens announced aren't doing immigration enforcement, they're just there to help with general security while TSA is so short-staffed.

This is what happens when you play politics with essential services. TSA agents are working without paychecks while Congress can't figure out funding for the Department of Homeland Security. Meanwhile, regular travelers are stuck dealing with the chaos. The whole situation is completely preventable but here we are, turning the world's busiest airport into a disaster zone.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents in tactical gear at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: Atlantic Station and Midtown Crack National Top 10 Best Places
stories.summary: Atlantic Station landed the #1 spot on Niche.com's Best Places to Live in America list, with Midtown coming in at #6. The rankings looked at schools, cost of living, jobs, crime, and amenities across 18,000 neighborhoods nationwide. Not bad for a city everyone says is getting too expensive.
stories.body:
Well this is interesting. Niche.com just dropped their 2026 Best Places to Live in America rankings, and Atlanta grabbed two spots in the top 10. Atlantic Station somehow claimed the #1 spot nationally, with Midtown following at #6. That's 20% of the top 10 going to ITP neighborhoods, which is pretty remarkable.

The rankings considered public schools, cost of living, job opportunities, crime rates, and local amenities across more than 18,000 neighborhoods nationwide. Atlantic Station makes some sense given the walkability, transit access, and all the development there over the past few years. Midtown has always been solid with the arts scene, restaurants, and job access.

What's funny is this comes at a time when everyone's complaining about Atlanta getting too expensive and losing its character. Maybe the national perspective is different, or maybe we're just used to complaining about our own backyard. Either way, it's nice to see the city getting recognition for something other than traffic and crime.
stories.imageAlt: Atlantic Station development and skyline view
